subject: maize / nutrient
question: A maize farmer's lower leaves are yellowing from the tip inward in a V-shape after heavy rain on sandy soil. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: Nitrogen deficiency
options.B: Phosphorus deficiency
options.C: Potassium deficiency

explanation: Nitrogen deficiency yellows the OLDEST lower leaves in a V from the tip, worsened by leaching on sandy soil after rain. Phosphorus shows purpling; potassium scorches leaf margins; waterlogging affects the whole plant.

subject: maize / pest
question: Young maize shows 'windowpane' patches on new leaves and moist sawdust-like frass in the whorl. Which pest is most likely?

options.A: Stem borer
options.B: Fall armyworm
options.C: Grasshoppers

explanation: Windowpane feeding plus wet frass in the whorl is the signature of fall armyworm. Stem borers tunnel stems, grasshoppers chew edges, termites attack near soil.
source_reference: verify: FAO fall armyworm IPM guide

subject: sorghum / striga
question: Wata ciyawa mai launin shunayya tana fitowa kusa da dawa wadda ta yi rauni duk da ƙasa tana da kyau. Mene ne mafi yiwuwa?

options.A: Ciyawa talakawa tana gasa kawai
options.B: Ruwa ya yi yawa a gona
options.C: Striga (witchweed) tana cin saiwar dawa
options.D: Furen dawa na yau da kullum

explanation: Striga is a purple-flowered parasitic weed that attaches to sorghum roots and stunts the crop even on good soil.

subject: maize / variety
question: In Kaduna the rains started two weeks late but are expected to be adequate overall. What is the soundest maize choice?

options.A: Plant a full-season variety as usual
options.B: Plant on the first light shower
options.C: Abandon maize for the whole year

explanation: A late start shortens the effective season, so an early-maturing variety matures in time. Full-season risks not finishing; planting on one shower or abandoning are poor calls.
source_reference: verify: IITA variety guide + ADP advisory

subject: poultry / brooding
question: What is the recommended brooder temperature near day-old chicks during the first week?

options.A: About 32-35 C
options.B: About 20-22 C
options.C: About 26-28 C

explanation: Week-one brooding temperature at chick level is about 32-35 C, reduced ~3 C per week. Lower is too cold; 40-42 C is too hot.

subject: poultry / brooding
question: Chicks are huddling tightly directly under the heat source. What does this most likely mean?

options.A: They are too hot
options.B: They are too cold
options.C: The temperature is correct

explanation: Huddling under the heat means too cold; scattering to the edges and panting means too hot; even spreading means correct.

subject: poultry / feeding
question: What is the correct broiler feed sequence from day-old to market?

options.A: Finisher, then grower, then starter
options.B: Layer mash from start to finish
options.C: Starter, then grower, then finisher
options.D: Grower feed from start to finish

explanation: Broilers go starter (high protein, early) -> grower -> finisher (weight gain before market). The others mis-stage or use the wrong feed.

subject: poultry / biosecurity
question: Yaya ya kamata ka yi da sabbin kaji da ka saya kafin ka hada su da garken?

options.A: Ka hada su nan da nan da sauran kajin
options.B: Ka hada su a daren farko kawai
options.C: Babu bukatar wani kariya ko kadan
options.D: Ka raba su na mako biyu kafin ka hada su

explanation: New birds should be quarantined ~2 weeks before mixing, to avoid importing disease into the flock. Immediate mixing defeats the purpose.

subject: poultry / Newcastle
question: Several chickens die suddenly; others show greenish diarrhoea and a twisted neck. Which disease is most likely?

options.A: Newcastle disease
options.B: Coccidiosis
options.C: Gumboro (IBD)

explanation: Sudden death + greenish diarrhoea + nervous signs (twisted neck) is classic Newcastle. Coccidiosis = bloody droppings; Gumboro = whitish diarrhoea; fowl pox = skin lesions.

subject: poultry / coccidiosis
question: Young birds on damp litter are droopy with ruffled feathers and bloody droppings. What is the most likely cause?

options.A: Newcastle disease
options.B: Coccidiosis
options.C: Gumboro (IBD)

explanation: Bloody droppings in young birds on wet litter point to coccidiosis (an Eimeria parasite). Newcastle/Gumboro have different signs; worms rarely cause frank blood like this.

subject: poultry / Gumboro
question: Birds around 4 weeks old show whitish watery diarrhoea, vent pecking and trembling, and the disease attacks their immune (bursal) organ. Which is it?

options.A: Newcastle disease
options.B: Fowl cholera
options.C: Gumboro (IBD)

explanation: Whitish watery diarrhoea, vent pecking and trembling in ~3-6 week birds attacking the bursa is Gumboro (infectious bursal disease).

subject: poultry / Newcastle
question: Mene ne kariya mafi muhimmanci daga cutar Newcastle a kaji?

options.A: Maganin antibiotic don warkar da ita
options.B: Ware kaji kawai ba tare da rigakafi
options.C: Babu wani abu da za a iya yi

explanation: Newcastle has no cure once it strikes; prevention is vaccination plus biosecurity. Antibiotics don't cure a virus; isolation alone is insufficient.

subject: planting / timing
question: When is it safest to plant maize at the start of the rainy season?

options.A: After two or three steady rains
options.B: On the very first single rain
options.C: Before any rain, in the dry season
options.D: Only after the rains have ended

explanation: Waiting for a stable onset (2-3 good rains) lets the soil hold moisture and avoids seedlings dying in an early dry spell. The others are mistimed or out of season.

subject: flooding / management
question: A field is prone to waterlogging during heavy rains. Which practice best protects the crop?

options.A: Add more water to balance the soil
options.B: Make ridges and provide drainage channels
options.C: Compact the soil so water sits on top
options.D: Remove all the topsoil from the field

explanation: Ridging and drainage move excess water away from roots. Adding water, compacting, or stripping topsoil all worsen waterlogging.

subject: heat stress / poultry
question: During intense heat, chickens are panting with open beaks. What is the best immediate action?

options.A: Add more heat inside the poultry house
options.B: Reduce the amount of drinking water
options.C: Give shade, ventilation and cool water

explanation: Open-beak panting is heat stress; provide shade, ventilation and cool water. Adding heat, cutting water, or blocking airflow make it worse.

subject: drought / adaptation
question: Rains have started late and are expected to be short this season. Which maize strategy best secures a harvest?

options.A: Plant a full-season variety as usual
options.B: Delay planting several more weeks
options.C: Plant the seed much deeper than normal

explanation: An early-maturing variety (~90 days) finishes before the rains cut off. Full-season may not mature; deeper planting and further delay don't fix a short season.

subject: storage / grain
question: What is the most reliable low-cost way to protect stored maize grain from weevils?

options.A: Dry it well and seal it in hermetic (PICS) bags
options.B: Store it loosely in an open basket
options.C: Keep it in a loosely tied ordinary sack
options.D: Sprinkle water on it to keep it cool

explanation: Drying to ~12-13% moisture then hermetic (PICS) storage starves weevils of oxygen, no chemicals needed. Airflow, moisture and loose sacks all let weevils thrive.

subject: harvest / rice
question: Yaushe ne lokaci mafi kyau na girbin shinkafa?

options.A: Lokacin da ganye har yanzu suke kore sosai
options.B: Lokacin da kusan kashi 80 na hatsi suka yi rawaya da tauri
options.C: Lokacin da hatsi suke da madara har yanzu
options.D: Nan da nan bayan shinkafa ta yi fure

explanation: Rice is ready when ~80% of grains are golden and hard. Green leaves, milky grains, and just-after-flowering all mean it is immature.

subject: seed / germination test
question: Before planting a whole field, a farmer tests 100 saved maize seeds and 75 sprout. What is the best action?

options.A: Plant at the normal rate, no change
options.B: Discard all the seed as useless
options.C: Plant slightly more thickly to compensate
options.D: Soak the seed in fertilizer first

explanation: A ~75% germination rate is usable but reduced, so plant slightly thicker to compensate; below ~70% you'd replace the seed. Normal rate risks a thin stand.

subject: feed / dry-season ration
question: To feed ruminants through the dry season when grass is scarce, which conserved residue gives the most protein?

options.A: Dry maize cob residue
options.B: Threshed rice straw
options.C: Cereal husk chaff

explanation: Legume haulms (groundnut, cowpea) are protein-rich and the best dry-season residue. Cob residue, rice straw and chaff are bulky but low in protein.
